ksas.statement: Knowledge of model interpretability and explainability methods, including SHAP values, LIME, partial dependence plots, and when explanation requirements should influence model selection.

Knowledge of AI governance structures, including model risk management, AI lifecycle oversight, accountability assignment, and the organizational conditions that enable responsible AI deployment at scale.
Knowledge of AI risk taxonomy, including model risk, algorithmic bias, opacity, adversarial vulnerability, data poisoning, training data integrity failures, and systemic AI failure modes.
Knowledge of responsible AI principles and their operationalization, including fairness metrics, explainability methods, human oversight requirements, and AI impact assessment methodologies.

Knowledge of AI regulatory frameworks, including the EU AI Act risk classification structure, conformity assessment requirements, prohibited use categories, and obligations on providers versus deployers.
Knowledge of AI governance framework components, including AI system registries, tiered review processes, impact assessment methodologies, and post-deployment monitoring requirements.
Knowledge of responsible AI principles and audit methods, including fairness metric selection, bias test design, explainability documentation standards, and human oversight protocols.
Knowledge of AI regulatory compliance requirements, including EU AI Act conformity assessment obligations, prohibited use categories, and documentation requirements for high-risk AI systems.

Knowledge of AI literacy concepts, including what AI is and is not, how ML models work at a conceptual level, AI limitations and failure modes, and responsible AI principles for non-technical audiences.
Knowledge of AI risk categories, including performance risk, bias and fairness risk, explainability risk, robustness risk, safety risk, privacy risk, and regulatory compliance risk.

Knowledge of algorithmic fairness concepts, including group fairness metrics (demographic parity, equalized odds, calibration), individual fairness, and the mathematical trade-offs between competing fairness definitions.
Knowledge of AI explainability methods, including SHAP, LIME, counterfactual explanations, attention visualization, and the appropriate application of each method type by model class.
Knowledge of AI regulatory requirements, including the EU AI Act prohibited use categories, high-risk system obligations, conformity assessment requirements, and technical documentation standards.

Knowledge of AI and machine learning-specific model risk considerations, including data drift, concept drift, black-box explainability requirements, and how traditional model risk frameworks must adapt for non-parametric, ensemble, and foundation models.
Skill in designing and operating enterprise data and AI governance councils, including role definition, decision rights, escalation paths, and policy enforcement mechanisms.
Skill in designing AI governance frameworks that address the full AI lifecycle from procurement or build through development, testing, deployment, monitoring, and retirement.
Skill in constructing and communicating AI risk assessments to non-technical executive and board audiences using business risk framing and financial impact language.
Skill in managing AI governance review processes, including intake, triage, stakeholder coordination, assessment execution, and decision documentation.
Skill in conducting AI impact assessments, including risk tiering, harm identification, bias evaluation, human oversight documentation, and residual risk determination.
Skill in designing and executing bias audits for AI systems, including test dataset construction, metric selection, results interpretation, and remediation recommendation.
Define and maintain the enterprise AI governance framework, including the AI system inventory, risk tiering, review thresholds, and decommissioning criteria.
Establish and maintain the enterprise AI inventory, cataloging all AI systems in production with risk tier, owner, training data provenance, model version, and review status.
Lead enterprise AI ethics reviews for high-stakes applications, including impact assessments, bias audits, and explainability documentation prior to production deployment.
Define the enterprise AI acceptable use policy, prohibited use categories, and human oversight requirements for AI-assisted decision making.
Maintain the enterprise AI system inventory, ensuring all AI applications are registered, risk-tiered, owned, and subject to appropriate review and monitoring.
Conduct AI impact assessments for new and materially modified AI systems prior to production deployment.
Manage ongoing AI policy compliance monitoring, including periodic review of deployed AI systems against current policy requirements and regulatory obligations.
Conduct AI impact assessments for new and materially modified AI systems, producing documented risk findings with recommended mitigations and residual risk statements.
Execute bias audits on AI systems in development and production, documenting methodology, findings, and recommended interventions.
Develop and maintain responsible AI assessment tools, templates, and rubrics for use across the enterprise AI development lifecycle.
Monitor AI regulatory developments and assess their implications for the enterprise AI system portfolio, producing compliance gap analyses for impacted systems.

Skill in designing human oversight mechanisms for AI-assisted decision-making, including escalation triggers, override procedures, human-in-the-loop protocols, and meaningful human control verification.

Skill in developing AI system documentation including model cards, system cards, and datasheets for datasets that meet both internal governance review and external regulatory conformity assessment requirements.

Ability to calibrate AI governance oversight proportionally to the actual risk level of AI applications, avoiding both under-governance of high-risk systems and excessive governance that impedes low-risk innovation.
Ability to navigate ethical trade-offs in AI deployment where competing values such as fairness versus accuracy, transparency versus privacy, or speed versus safety require principled resolution.
Ability to build organizational commitment to responsible AI practices by demonstrating their business value and risk reduction benefits rather than relying solely on compliance mandates.
Ability to translate emerging AI ethics research, evolving societal expectations, and new regulatory guidance into practical governance policy updates that maintain organizational alignment.
